まぜそば 麻婆 卵入り

english: dry ramen mapo tofu style with egg
japanese: まぜそば 麻婆 卵入り

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for mazesoba or mapo mazesoba. FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0GXM (Noodle dishes) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for mazesoba or mapo-style dry noodles.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for mazesoba or mapo noodle dish. SNOMED CT coverage for specific Japanese noodle preparations is absent.

preparation_method: topped with soft-boiled or poached egg

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Ken Mapo Mazesoba with Egg as served at a Japanese ramen restaurant, approximately 420 g including noodles, mapo-style sauce, tofu, ground pork, and one egg.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite: cooked wheat noodles, ground pork, tofu, egg, and soy-based sauce components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods and SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for mazesoba or mapo mazesoba exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries: cooked wheat noodles (FDC 168917, ~50% by weight), ground pork cooked (FDC 167903, ~15%), firm tofu (FDC 172475, ~15%), whole egg cooked (FDC 173424, ~10%), and soy-sesame sauce components (~10%). Proportions reflect a typical mazesoba bowl composition. Values are per 100 g of the combined dish.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of wheat noodles and mixed noodle dishes — analogous estimate based on cooked wheat noodles (GI ~47, glucose reference)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mazesoba or mapo mazesoba. GI of 47 is adopted from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked wheat noodles (boiled, ~47, glucose scale), which is the dominant carbohydrate component of this dish. The mapo sauce, egg, pork, and tofu components contribute protein and fat that further attenuate postprandial glycemic response, so GI 47 is a conservative upper-bound estimate. GL per serving calculated as (47 × 81.9 g available carbohydrate per 420 g serving) / 100 = 38.5, rounded to 38.9 using per-100g carbohydrate of 19.5 g × 4.2 × 47/100.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g; ~2184 mg per bowl)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Ken Mapo Mazesoba due to its very high sodium load from doubanjiang, soy sauce, and seasoning, which can significantly exceed the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg in a single serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from wheat noodles, partially offset by protein, fat, and fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Ken Mapo Mazesoba in controlled portions, as the wheat noodle base delivers a moderate glycemic load (~39 per bowl); pairing with the egg and tofu protein content helps blunt postprandial glucose rise, but portion control and blood glucose monitoring are advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium and moderate saturated fat from ground pork and egg yolk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should exercise caution with Ken Mapo Mazesoba given its high sodium content (~2184 mg per serving) and moderate saturated fat (~7.6 g per serving), both of which are associated with elevated blood pressure and adverse lipid profiles; occasional consumption with reduced-sodium modifications is preferable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ken Mapo Mazesoba with Egg is a Japanese dry mixed noodle dish (mazesoba) inspired by the Sichuan Chinese mapo tofu flavour profile. Unlike traditional ramen, mazesoba is served without broth — the noodles are drained and tossed directly with a rich, spicy, umami-laden sauce. The mapo-style sauce is built from doubanjiang (spicy fermented bean paste), soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, ginger, and Sichuan peppercorn, creating a bold, numbing heat characteristic of mapo preparations. Ground pork and silken or firm tofu are folded into the sauce, and the dish is crowned with a soft-boiled or poached egg that adds richness and creaminess when broken into the noodles. The noodles themselves are thick, chewy wheat noodles typical of ramen-style preparations. The overall flavour is intensely savoury, spicy, and slightly numbing, with a silky mouthfeel from the egg yolk and tofu. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~168 kcal per 100 g), meaningful protein from pork, tofu, and egg, and moderate carbohydrate from wheat noodles. Sodium content is high due to the fermented paste and soy sauce base, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 47 (low GI) based on the wheat noodle component, though the glycemic load per full bowl is moderate to high. This dish is served at Japanese ramen restaurants in Singapore, including Ramen Matsuri, and represents a fusion of Japanese mazesoba technique with Sichuan mapo tofu flavouring.
common_user_queries: Is mazesoba the same as ramen?
How many calories in mapo mazesoba?
What is in mapo mazesoba sauce?
Is mapo mazesoba high in sodium?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for mazesoba or mapo mazesoba exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted USDA SR Legacy composite of the dish's known main components. GI is estimated by analogy from the University of Sydney GI Database wheat noodle entry. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the dish composition is well-characterised from the restaurant menu description.
notes: Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked wheat noodles, ground pork, firm tofu, whole egg, and soy-sesame sauce. No direct approved-source entry for mazesoba or mapo mazesoba was found. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy composite used for this estimation. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamins, and minerals are estimated from the USDA component breakdown and are approximate. Sodium is estimated conservatively at 520 mg per 100 g based on the high-sodium sauce components; actual restaurant sodium may be higher. GI of 47 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database wheat noodle entry; no direct GI measurement for mazesoba exists in the approved GI literature. The food_group has been corrected from the input value of 'Dairy and eggs' to 'Grains and staples' as this is a noodle-based dish. The input food_group assignment appears to have been an error in the seed record. Serving size of 420 g is estimated based on typical Japanese ramen restaurant bowl portions.

q1_food_identity.notes: Canonical English name 'Ken Mapo Mazesoba with Egg' is precise and non-promotional. Japanese local name provided as required by G6 for Japanese cuisine.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: All four core macros (energy_kcal 168.0, protein_g 8.5, fat_g 6.2, carbohydrate_g 19.5) are present, numeric, and non-zero. Source is USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite, clearly documented in nutrient_source.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (1.2), sodium_mg (520.0), saturated_fat_g (1.8), and sugar_g (1.8) are all present as numeric values and are plausible for this dish type.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (47), gi_category ('low'), glycemic_load_per_serving (38.9), and gi_source are all present. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database wheat noodle entry, clearly documented.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish, closest_parent) and FoodEx2 A0GXM (Noodle dishes, closest_parent) are defensible anchors. SNOMED CT is null with documented gap note. No codes are invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters, explains what the food is, preparation method, taste and texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutritional profile.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern, sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate concern, glycemic load), Cardiovascular Disease (high concern, sodium and saturated fat). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases list is comprehensive with 11 entries covering English variants, Japanese script, and romanized forms. aliases_by_language is populated for English, Japanese, and romanized. All strings in aliases_by_language are present verbatim in the flat aliases list. Consistency check passed.
